questions.stem: In consumer theory, utility is best defined as which of the following?
options: The cost of producing a good
The money price of a good
The weight of a good purchased
The satisfaction or benefit a consumer gets from consuming goods and services
questions.explanation: Utility is the satisfaction or wellbeing a consumer derives from consuming goods and services. Consumer theory assumes people try to maximise their total utility given their budget.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Marginal utility is best defined as which of the following?
options: The extra satisfaction from consuming one more unit of a good
The total satisfaction from all units consumed
The satisfaction lost when a good is taxed
The average satisfaction per cedi spent
questions.explanation: Marginal utility is the addition to total utility from consuming one more unit of a good. It is the change in total utility divided by the change in quantity consumed.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The law of diminishing marginal utility states that, as more of a good is consumed, the marginal utility from each extra unit will eventually do what?
options: Fall
Become infinite
Stay exactly constant
Rise without limit
questions.explanation: Beyond some point, each additional unit consumed adds less to total satisfaction than the previous one, so marginal utility falls as consumption increases. This is the law of diminishing marginal utility.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: While marginal utility is positive but falling, what is happening to total utility?
options: Total utility is negative
Total utility is constant
Total utility is still rising, but at a decreasing rate
Total utility is falling
questions.explanation: As long as marginal utility is positive, each extra unit still adds to the total, so total utility keeps rising. Because marginal utility is falling, the total rises more slowly with each unit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total utility reaches its maximum at the point where marginal utility is which of the following?
options: Equal to price
Negative
Equal to zero
At its highest
questions.explanation: Total utility stops rising and is at a maximum when the last unit consumed adds nothing more, that is when marginal utility equals zero. Consuming beyond that point would make marginal utility negative and reduce total utility.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The equimarginal principle (the condition for consumer equilibrium with marginal utility) states that a consumer maximises utility when which condition holds for all goods?
options: The marginal utility per cedi (or per unit of money) spent is equal across all goods
Total utility is equal across goods
The quantity bought of every good is equal
The price of every good is equal
questions.explanation: Utility is maximised when the ratio of marginal utility to price, $MU/P$, is the same for every good. If it were higher for one good, the consumer could gain by reallocating spending towards that good.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If for good A the marginal utility per cedi is higher than for good B, a utility maximising consumer should do which of the following?
options: Buy equal amounts of both
Buy more of A and less of B
Buy less of A and more of B
Stop buying both goods
questions.explanation: When $MU_A/P_A$ exceeds $MU_B/P_B$, each cedi spent on A yields more satisfaction. The consumer should shift spending towards A. As more A is bought its marginal utility falls, moving the ratios back towards equality.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The downward slope of the demand curve can be explained by diminishing marginal utility because consumers will only buy extra units if which of the following happens?
options: Advertising increases
Their income rises
The price of substitutes rises
The price falls to match the lower marginal utility of extra units
questions.explanation: Because marginal utility falls as more is consumed, a consumer values each extra unit less. They are only willing to buy more units if the price falls to match that lower marginal value, which gives a downward sloping demand curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An indifference curve shows which of the following?
options: The cheapest combination of two goods
All combinations of two goods that give the consumer the same level of satisfaction
All combinations the consumer can just afford
The most preferred single good
questions.explanation: An indifference curve joins all combinations of two goods among which the consumer is indifferent, that is which yield equal total utility. The consumer ranks every point on one curve as equally desirable.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Indifference curves are normally drawn convex to the origin. This reflects which idea?
options: A fixed money income
Increasing marginal utility
A diminishing marginal rate of substitution between the two goods
Constant prices
questions.explanation: The convex shape reflects a diminishing marginal rate of substitution: as a consumer has more of one good, they are willing to give up less and less of the other to gain a further unit of the first.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The marginal rate of substitution (MRS) between two goods measures which of the following?
options: The ratio of the prices of the two goods
The consumer's total income
The rate at which a consumer will trade one good for the other while staying equally satisfied
The total utility from both goods
questions.explanation: The MRS is the amount of one good a consumer is willing to give up to obtain one more unit of the other while remaining on the same indifference curve. It equals the slope of the indifference curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Why can two indifference curves for the same consumer never cross?
options: Because income is limited
Because crossing would imply two different utility levels at the same point, contradicting consistent preferences
Because the budget line is straight
Because prices are fixed
questions.explanation: Each indifference curve represents one utility level. If two curves crossed, the point of intersection would lie on both, implying it gives two different levels of satisfaction at once. That contradicts the assumption of consistent preferences.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A budget line (budget constraint) shows which of the following?
options: The marginal utility of each good
All combinations giving equal utility
The total cost of production
All combinations of two goods a consumer can buy with a given income at given prices
questions.explanation: The budget line shows the maximum combinations of two goods a consumer can purchase given a fixed money income and the prices of the goods. Points on the line use up the whole budget.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The slope of the budget line is determined by which of the following?
options: The consumer's tastes
The marginal rate of substitution
The ratio of the prices of the two goods
The level of total utility
questions.explanation: The budget line's slope equals the ratio of the two prices (the relative price of the good on the horizontal axis). It shows the rate at which the market lets the consumer trade one good for the other.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A rise in the consumer's money income, with prices unchanged, will do what to the budget line?
options: Leave it unchanged
Make it steeper
Make it flatter
Shift it outward (to the right), parallel to the original
questions.explanation: A higher income lets the consumer buy more of both goods, so the budget line shifts outward. Since relative prices are unchanged, the slope is the same and the new line is parallel to the old one.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Using indifference curves and a budget line, the consumer's optimum (equilibrium) is found at the point where which condition holds?
options: Two indifference curves cross
Total utility is zero
The budget line cuts the highest indifference curve twice
The budget line is tangent to the highest attainable indifference curve
questions.explanation: The best the consumer can do is reach the highest indifference curve the budget allows. That occurs where the budget line just touches (is tangent to) an indifference curve, so the curve's slope equals the price ratio.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: At the consumer's optimum using indifference analysis, the marginal rate of substitution equals which of the following?
options: The consumer's total income
The ratio of the prices of the two goods
Zero
The sum of the two marginal utilities
questions.explanation: At the tangency point the slope of the indifference curve (the MRS) equals the slope of the budget line (the price ratio). So in equilibrium MRS equals the ratio of prices.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The difference between total utility and marginal utility is best summarised as which of the following?
options: Total utility is the overall satisfaction from all units; marginal utility is the extra satisfaction from the last unit
Total utility applies only to free goods
They are the same thing measured in different units
Marginal utility is always larger than total utility
questions.explanation: Total utility is the sum of satisfaction from all units consumed. Marginal utility is the addition to that total from the last unit. Marginal utility is the slope, or rate of change, of the total utility curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'diamond and water paradox' (why water is cheap but diamonds dear despite water being essential) is explained chiefly by which idea?
options: Price reflects marginal utility, and water is so abundant that its marginal utility is low
Diamonds are heavier than water
Diamonds are a necessity
Water has no total utility
questions.explanation: Water has enormous total utility but, because it is plentiful, the marginal utility of an extra unit is low, so its price is low. Diamonds are scarce, so their marginal utility and price are high. Price tracks marginal, not total, utility.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Consumer surplus is best defined as which of the following?
options: The producer's profit
The amount a consumer spends in total
The difference between what a consumer is willing to pay and what they actually pay
The tax paid on a good
questions.explanation: Consumer surplus is the extra benefit consumers receive when the price they pay is below the maximum they would have been willing to pay. It is the gap between total willingness to pay and total actual expenditure.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a consumer's total utility from 3 units is 30 and from 4 units is 36, the marginal utility of the fourth unit is:
questions.explanation: Marginal utility of the fourth unit is the change in total utility, $36 - 30 = 6$. It is the extra satisfaction the fourth unit adds.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: An ordinal approach to utility (as used in indifference curve analysis) assumes that consumers can do which of the following?
options: Never compare any two bundles
Only compare goods of equal price
Measure utility in exact numerical units called utils
Rank bundles of goods in order of preference without assigning exact numbers
questions.explanation: The ordinal approach only requires consumers to rank bundles as better, worse, or equally good, not to measure satisfaction in cardinal units. Indifference curve analysis rests on this weaker, more realistic assumption.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A fall in the price of one good, with income and the other price unchanged, will do what to the budget line?
options: Make it vertical
Leave it unchanged
Pivot it outward along the axis of the good that became cheaper
Shift it inward parallel to the original
questions.explanation: When one good becomes cheaper, the consumer can buy more of it for the same income, so the budget line pivots outward along that good's axis. The intercept for the other good, whose price is unchanged, stays put.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which assumption about consumer preferences underlies standard indifference curve analysis?
options: Preferences are inconsistent and change randomly
Preferences are consistent (transitive) and more of a good is preferred to less
Consumers prefer less to more of every good
All goods are identical
questions.explanation: Standard analysis assumes preferences are complete and transitive (consistent), and that more of a desirable good is preferred to less (non satiation). These assumptions give the familiar downward sloping, non crossing indifference curves.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the marginal utility of a good is negative, consuming an additional unit will do what to total utility?
options: Reduce total utility
Leave total utility unchanged
Make marginal utility positive
Increase total utility
questions.explanation: Negative marginal utility means the extra unit subtracts from total satisfaction, for example over consumption to the point of discomfort. A rational consumer would not pay to consume units with negative marginal utility.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A consumer has fixed income and faces fixed prices. Which combination of two goods does the consumer actually choose?
options: The bundle with the most units of the cheaper good
The bundle where total spending is smallest
The bundle on the budget line that reaches the highest attainable indifference curve
Any point inside the budget line
questions.explanation: A utility maximising consumer chooses the affordable bundle that lies on the highest indifference curve they can reach. That is the tangency point between the budget line and an indifference curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
